query: nail changes in psoriasis vs other dermatological disorders

retrieved_docs.title: Nail Psoriasis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Many conditions need to be distinguished from nail psoriasis. Because nail dystrophy and other characteristic findings in nail psoriasis can be found in other disease states, distinguishing these can be challenging. For instance, age-related changes from trauma, vascular lower extremity diseases (eg, peripheral artery disease, chronic venous stasis, trauma), and peripheral neuropathy can cause nail changes that may be confused for nail psoriasis; however, these findings will be unlikely to show other findings of psoriasis if they are the cause of nail changes. [13] Many medications can also cause nail changes, so ruling out medication changes is essential. [13] Some other pertinent differential diagnoses with nail changes most similar to nail psoriasis are listed below.

retrieved_docs.title: Nail Psoriasis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Many other dermatologic conditions may also present with nail findings that can overlap with nail psoriasis, including atopic dermatitis, Darier disease, and Hailey-Hailey disease. These conditions should also be considered when evaluating a patient with nail changes. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Nail Psoriasis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pityriasis rubra pilaris: Though nail changes in pityriasis rubra pilaris can include thickened nails with splinter hemorrhages, which are less common findings in nail psoriasis, the cutaneous findings of this disease can be similar to those in psoriasis. Skin findings in pityriasis rubra pilaris, though, will usually include hyperkeratotic follicular papules, orange-red plaques with fine scales, and hyperkeratosis of the palms and soles. [9] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Nail Psoriasis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Nail psoriasis can manifest clinically as a wide variety of nail changes, like nail discoloration (eg, oil drops), subungual hyperkeratosis, pitting (ie, punctate nail depressions), and onycholysis (ie, distal nail plate separation from nail bed), depending on the part of the nail unit affected (see Image . Nail Psoriasis). Patients with nail psoriasis may have impaired quality of life due to the appearance of their nails, and significant morbidity and functional impairments may arise in some cases. [4] [5] Nail psoriasis management is challenging since prolonged treatment is required and may not be able to control disease activity entirely. Educating patients on the prognosis and outcomes of treatment is most important to set expectations and prepare for a successful treatment approach. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Onychoscopy -- Clinical Significance -- Inflammatory Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Onychoscopic examination of the proximal nail fold reveals a reduced mean capillary count and architectural alterations, including coiled capillaries and dropouts. On onychoscopic examination of the hyponychium, irregularly distributed, elongated, tortuous, and dilated capillaries are observed, similar to those seen in psoriasis of the skin. The capillary density correlates directly with the severity of disease and treatment response. In some cases, the capillaries, which appear as red dots, can be visible on the proximal nail fold. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Plaque Psoriasis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Nail changes are common, including pitting, onycholysis, subungual hyperkeratosis, and oil-drop discoloration, the latter of which is often described as a salmon patch appearance. Nail involvement is a strong predictor of concomitant psoriatic arthritis (see Image . Nails in Psoriasis).

retrieved_docs.title: Onychoscopy -- Clinical Significance -- Inflammatory Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Onychoscopy has markedly improved the visualization of minute features in nail psoriasis. [79] Nail pitting, splinter hemorrhages, and onycholysis are the most common findings encountered across multiple studies. [80] [81] Ultimately, the involvement of the nail matrix results in large, deep, irregularly arranged pits (versus the small, shallow, and regularly arranged pits in alopecia areata) grossly. Leukonychia is also often present. The involvement of the nail bed results in onycholysis, salmon patches, splinter hemorrhages, and subungual hyperkeratosis. [81]

retrieved_docs.title: Nail Psoriasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Determine the etiology, pathophysiology, and exacerbating factors of nail psoriasis. Assess history and physical examination findings of nail psoriasis. Identify the management options for nail psoriasis according to the severity of the disease. Coordinate the interprofessional team to improve outcomes for patients with nail psoriasis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Nail -- Clinical Significance -- Diseases of the Nails and Associated Histopathologic Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Psoriasis : Hypergranulosis in nail matrix and bed, hypogranulosis in hyponychium, neutrophil exocytosis (Munro microabscesses), hyperkeratosis, parakeratosis, spongiosis

retrieved_docs.title: Nail Psoriasis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with nail psoriasis have a chronic and protracted course with periods of improvement and worsening, which can impact quality of life. Patients with nail psoriasis may have more difficulty with treatment and thus poorer disease control, since there are limited data on treatments compared with cutaneous psoriasis. [65] Worsening of nail disease may occur due to nail trauma, which is often unavoidable, due to koebnerization. However, treatment with topical or systemic agents for mild and moderate-to-severe disease, respectively, may alter the disease process, though with some concern for adverse effects. [66]

retrieved_docs.title: Nail Psoriasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Nail psoriasis is a subtype of psoriasis, the common chronic inflammatory skin condition that can also affect the joints. Although cutaneous manifestations of psoriasis are frequently observed, nail involvement significantly impacts patients' social and psychological well-being. This activity comprehensively reviews the clinical presentation, evaluation, and treatment strategies for nail psoriasis, shedding light on the challenges posed by the slow growth pace of nails and the prolonged nature of treatment. The session emphasizes the crucial role of the interprofessional healthcare team in evaluating and treating patients with nail psoriasis, highlighting the need for collaborative approaches to enhance patient outcomes. Learners will gain insights into the diverse clinical manifestations of nail psoriasis, including discoloration, hyperkeratosis, pitting, and onycholysis, equipping them with the knowledge to navigate the complexities of managing this condition. Educating clinicians on setting realistic patient expectations and fostering effective communication within the healthcare team are pivotal aspects addressed in this activity, aiming to improve the quality of care provided to individuals grappling with psoriasis of the nails.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Nail -- Clinical Significance -- Diseases of the Nails and Associated Histopathologic Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Melanocytic activation : No increase in melanocyte density, increase in pigment [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Nail Psoriasis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Psoriasis is a common chronic inflammatory skin condition; however, a subtype of psoriasis can include nail involvement, systemic involvement (eg, joints), or both nail and systemic involvement; nail psoriasis can be the first presentation of cutaneous psoriasis, a sequela of cutaneous psoriasis, or a concurrent presentation of psoriasis. [1] Psoriatic involvement of the nail bed or nail matrix is the purported cause of nail psoriasis. [2] Nail involvement is a visible indicator to predict concomitant or future inflammatory joint activity, most prominently in the distal interphalangeal joint, where psoriatic arthritis classically presents. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Nail Psoriasis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Nail psoriasis affects both children and adults, affecting both males and females equally, with increasing prevalence with increasing age. [9] [10] Nail psoriasis mostly develops in association with cutaneous psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis, with studies varying widely on their prevalence; however, a strong association of 80% to 90% between psoriatic arthritis and nail psoriasis is found across multiple studies, likely because psoriatic arthritis classically affects the distal interphalangeal joint, which is the joint closest to the nail. [11] Nail psoriasis may develop as a sole manifestation of psoriasis or concurrently with other symptoms or after the onset of cutaneous symptoms. [6]

retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal diseases: Inflammatory bowel disease, celiac disease, amoebiasis, ascariasis, and lymphoma of the gastrointestinal tract

retrieved_docs.title: Paronychia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: 5- Nail Psoriasis - psoriasis can also affect the fingernails and toenails. It may cause thickening of the nails with areas of pitting, ridges, irregular contour, and even raising of the nail from the nail bed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1812
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV) are classified as plain corticosteroid dermatological preparations, indicating that they represent a highly potent subgroup of topical steroid formulations used in dermatology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1965
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid dermatological preparations, plain encompass a range of topical corticosteroid strengths that are classified by potency groups. Within this category, “Corticosteroids, weak (group I)” represent the low‑potency end of the spectrum, while “Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV)” represent the high‑potency end. This hierarchical classification allows clinicians to select an appropriate plain corticosteroid preparation based on the severity of the skin condition, aiming to achieve therapeutic effectiveness while minimizing the risk of adverse effects associated with higher‑potency steroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1299

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: Benzoyl peroxide and methotrexate are both classified as dermatological substances, indicating that each is used in the treatment of skin conditions. As members of the same therapeutic category, these agents share relevance in dermatology, where they are employed for their specific pharmacologic actions on the skin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2747
retrieved_docs.content: Haloprogin is categorized under the therapeutic class of Dermatological Agents, indicating that it is a medication specifically used for treating skin‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1639
retrieved_docs.content: Dermatological Agents [TC] is a therapeutic category that includes distinct therapeutic classes such as haloprogin and selenium sulfide. Both haloprogin and selenium sulfide are identified as therapeutic classes within Dermatological Agents [TC], reflecting the variety of agents used to manage skin disorders under this broader dermatological classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1246
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, weak (group I), Corticosteroids, potent (group III), and Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV) are all classified as plain corticosteroid dermatological preparations. These three potency groups represent increasing levels of anti‑inflammatory strength, allowing clinicians to select the appropriate topical corticosteroid formulation based on the severity of the skin condition and the desired therapeutic effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2585
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPSORIATICS constitute a therapeutic class of drugs used to treat psoriasis, and antipsoriatics for systemic use are a specific subset of this class that are administered orally, intravenously, or by other routes to exert effects throughout the body rather than locally on the skin. This relationship indicates that systemic antipsoriatic agents are encompassed within the broader category of antipsoriatics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE represent a therapeutic class that includes both ANTIFUNGALS FOR TOPICAL USE and ANTIFUNGALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE. By definition, topical antifungal agents are applied directly to the skin, nails, or scalp to treat superficial fungal infections, whereas systemic antifungal agents are administered orally or intravenously to reach deeper or more extensive dermatologic sites. The inclusion of both topical and systemic formulations under the umbrella of ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE reflects the clinical reality that dermatologic fungal conditions can be managed with either local therapy for mild or localized disease or systemic therapy for more severe, widespread, or refractory infections. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of selecting the appropriate route of administration based on infection severity, pathogen susceptibility, and patient factors, ensuring comprehensive care for dermatologic fungal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
retrieved_docs.content: are classified as plain dermatological preparations, representing distinct potency subclasses of simple topical steroids. Conversely, “Corticosteroids, weak, other combinations”, “Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations”, and “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations” are subclasses of “corticosteroids, other combinations”, indicating formulation with additional agents rather than as stand‑alone preparations. The hierarchical organization of potency groups (weak, moderately potent, potent, very potent) and formulation types (plain vs. combination) clarifies how hydrokortison and related corticosteroids are positioned across therapeutic protocols, dermatologic products, and combination drug regimens, underscoring their central role in the management of inflammatory skin conditions and related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE constitute a broad class of antifungal agents employed in the treatment of skin disorders, and within this class, antifungals for topical use represent the specific subset of medications applied directly to the skin surface to manage superficial fungal infections. Recognizing that topical antifungals are a component of the larger dermatological antifungal category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the site and severity of infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2729
retrieved_docs.content: Etretinát is classified as a keratolytika, meaning it belongs to the group of keratolytic agents that promote the shedding of the outer keratinized layer of the skin. As a synthetic retinoid, etretinát is used in dermatological practice for conditions that benefit from enhanced epidermal turnover, such as severe psoriasis and other hyperkeratotic skin disorders. Its inclusion in the keratolytika class underscores its mechanism of action in facilitating exfoliation and normalizing skin cell differentiation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2582
retrieved_docs.content: Acitretin is a member of the class of retinoids that are used for the treatment of psoriasis.

retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason, a potent glucocorticoid, is commonly employed in the management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), where it helps to diminish inflammation and improve the characteristic skin lesions of the disease. This therapeutic link highlights the important role of corticosteroid agents such as betamethason in treating inflammatory dermatologic conditions.
